title: 阳光下的罪恶
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\117-阳光下的罪恶-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot reveals his investigation into the murder of Eleanor Marshall. He first presents evidence found in the room of Eleanor's stepdaughter, Linda Marshall, including melted wax, burnt hair, and a book on witchcraft. Poirot suggests Linda may have performed a superstitious ritual using a wax effigy and a needle to express a desire to kill. He notes that Linda's alibi relies on time evidence she provided herself and suggests she could have traveled from the mountain to the beach to strangle Eleanor. Poirot also notes Linda's physical strength and her mother's past murder trial.

When Kenneth Marshall insists his wife is innocent, Poirot explores other motives, including blackmail, drug trafficking, or religious fanaticism. He proposes that Kenneth might have committed the murder with an accomplice, suggesting a partner could have typed a forged letter in his room to provide an alibi. Poirot exposes that Kenneth and Rosamund Darnley lied about their whereabouts and the positioning of a typewriter to cover their actions.

Finally, Poirot turns his attention to Patrick Redfern. He argues that Eleanor Marshall went to Elfrida Bay to meet a lover, believing it to be Patrick. Poirot asserts that Patrick killed Eleanor after she emerged from a hiding spot. He reveals that the "body" seen by witnesses was actually Patrick's accomplice, Christine, who was painted black and wearing a paper hat to impersonate the corpse. Poirot identifies Patrick and Christine as Edward and Christine Corey, noting they were recognized by police from a guest photograph following their involvement in the previous murder of Alice Corey.
